ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general hot and sour soup entry; no vegetarian-specific or Indian-restaurant variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers soups and broths as a category; no specific hot and sour soup or vegetarian variant code exists at finer granularity.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for vegetarian hot and sour soup as a food item.

main_ingredients: egg (optional in some versions; omitted in vegan variants)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of vegetarian hot and sour soup is approximately 300 g (one bowl), as typically served at Indian-Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and sour soup, vegetable-based — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indian-restaurant vegetarian hot and sour soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA branded foods. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Soup, hot and sour, Chinese restaurant' (FDC ID 173563) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN 2017 vegetable soup data, adjusted for vegetarian formulation (no meat, tofu substituted). Per-100g values represent a moderate-sodium, starch-thickened vegetable soup profile.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable soups and starch-thickened broths — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable-based soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian hot and sour soup. GI estimated at 35 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable soups (GI range 25–45) and starch-thickened broths. The low GI reflects the high water content, moderate vinegar acidity (which lowers postprandial glucose response), and low net carbohydrate density. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 13.5) / 100 ≈ 4.7, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 4.5 × 3 = 13.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1140 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this soup due to its high sodium content from soy sauce and broth; a single serving may contribute approximately 50–57% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load per serving (~4.7)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vegetarian hot and sour soup has a low GI and low GL, making it a generally suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion size and total meal carbohydrate load should still be monitored, and the high sodium content warrants attention in those with comorbid hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content offset by low saturated fat and plant-based ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the low saturated fat content and plant-based ingredients are cardioprotective, the high sodium content (~1140 mg per serving) is a significant concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease and should be factored into daily sodium budgeting.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Hot and Sour Soup is a plant-based adaptation of the classic Indo-Chinese hot and sour soup, commonly served at Indian-Chinese restaurants in Singapore such as Zaffron Tandoori. The soup is prepared by simmering a seasoned vegetable broth with tofu, mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and julienned vegetables, then thickening the liquid with cornstarch to achieve a silky, slightly viscous consistency. The characteristic flavour profile is a balance of sharp acidity from vinegar and heat from white pepper and chilli, creating the signature 'hot and sour' sensation. The dish is served hot and is typically garnished with spring onions and a drizzle of sesame oil. Nutritionally, it is low in calories (~35 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides modest protein from tofu. Its primary clinical concern is its high sodium content, which can reach approximately 1140 mg per standard 300 g bowl, making it unsuitable for individuals on strict sodium-restricted diets. The low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load make it appropriate for blood sugar management. It is suitable for vegetarians and is generally halal-compliant. The dish is a popular starter or light meal option in Singapore's Indian-Chinese restaurant segment.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indian-restaurant vegetarian hot and sour soup was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA branded foods. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy hot and sour soup data and ICMR-NIN vegetable soup data, adjusted for vegetarian formulation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database vegetable soup entries. Confidence is medium due to the estimation basis.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173563, 'Soup, hot and sour, Chinese restaurant') and ICMR-NIN 2017 vegetable soup data, adjusted for vegetarian formulation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based soup with no hydrogenated fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal-derived ingredients in vegetarian formulation). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food with no B12-containing ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no fortified or animal-derived ingredients present). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data and are approximate. GI set to 35 as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database vegetable soup entries; no direct measurement available. Served at Zaffron Tandoori - Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data source). Price SGD 10.90. This record requires laboratory verification for precise nutrient values.
